extracted_json.title: Step 3: Determine the Attractiveness of the Industry as a Whole

blocks.text: Step 3 : Determine the attractiveness of the industry as a whole
Def : The attractiveness (i.e., profit potential as measured by the ROIC) is determined by the cumulative (or total) strength of the 5 forces
If the cumulative strength of the 5 forces is high (H), then the industry is not attractive to new entrants or substitutes

Example : From Table 1, which shows the qualitative strength of each force (in column 3), the cumulative (or total) strength of the 5 forces in the PC industry in the 1980s is "medium (M) to high (H)"
⟹ PC industry (of the 1980s) is (probably) not attractive to new entrants or substitutes.

extracted_json.summary_topic_line: Determining overall industry attractiveness from the cumulative strength of the five forces (with PC-industry example).
extracted_json.summary_gist: Step 3 of industry analysis is determining the overall attractiveness (profit potential as measured by ROIC) of an industry, rated qualitatively as High, Medium, or Low based on the cumulative strength of the five forces. High cumulative strength means the industry is not attractive to new entrants or substitutes. Example: the PC industry of the 1980s had medium-to-high cumulative force strength, so it was probably not attractive.

transcription:
Step 3 : Determine the attractiveness of
            the industry as a whole
                Qualitative : H, M, L

Def :   The attractiveness (i.e., profit potential
        as measured by the ROIC) is determined
        by the cumulative (or total) strength
        of the 5 forces

        If the cumulative strength of the 5 forces
        is high (H), then the industry is not
        attractive to new entrants or substitutes

Example :   From Table 1, which shows the
            qualitative strength of each force
            (in column 3), the cumulative (or
            total) strength of the 5 forces in
            the PC industry in the 1980s is
            " medium (M) to high (H) "

        ⟹ PC industry (of the 1980s) is (probably)
           not attractive to new entrants or
           substitutes.
